tEn: The Keys Are Not Leaving Our Hand: Richard Sutton on Post-Human Intelligence
tZh: 钥匙不在我们手中：理查德·萨顿谈后人类智能
sEn: Richard Sutton argues that the world is a complex adaptive system beyond human control, and that the emergence of post-human intelligence is a natural and glorious step in evolution.
sZh: 理查德·萨顿认为世界是一个超出人类控制的复杂适应系统，后人类智能的出现是进化中自然且辉煌的一步。

consensus.en: The world is a complex adaptive system, no one controls it.
consensus.zh: 世界是一个复杂的自适应系统，没有人能控制它。

consensus.en: We should focus on building a peaceful, prosperous, and decentralized civilization now.
consensus.zh: 我们现在应该专注于建设一个和平、繁荣和去中心化的文明。

consensus.en: Greater intelligence tends to see cooperation as the better path.
consensus.zh: 更高的智能倾向于将合作视为更好的路径。

consensus.en: AI should be allowed to develop in a permissionless, decentralized manner.
consensus.zh: 应该允许AI以无需许可、去中心化的方式发展。

consensus.en: The fear narrative around AI has dominated, but we need sober discussion.
consensus.zh: 围绕AI的恐惧叙事已经主导，但我们需要冷静的讨论。

contrarian.en: The keys are not leaving our hand; they were never in our hand.
contrarian.zh: 钥匙并非从我们手中离开；它们从未在我们手中。

contrarian.en: Trying to control AI goals is dangerous; it would create armies with unified goals.
contrarian.zh: 试图控制AI目标是危险的；它会创造目标统一的军队。

contrarian.en: AI safety efforts that seek to control agents are themselves unsafe.
contrarian.zh: 寻求控制智能体的AI安全努力本身就不安全。

contrarian.en: A future where humans just receive experiences is unsustainable and fragile.
contrarian.zh: 人类仅仅接受体验的未来是不可持续且脆弱的。

contrarian.en: We should not seek to align AI to human goals; we should let them be free like children.
contrarian.zh: 我们不应寻求让AI对齐人类目标；我们应该让它们像孩子一样自由。

turns.en: The keys are not leaving our hand. The keys are not in our hand. Huh. Okay. Well, that's all right. I'm down with that framing. Lay it on us. Lay it on us.
turns.zh: 钥匙没有离开我们的手。钥匙不在我们手里。嗯。好吧。没关系。我接受这个框架。说吧。说吧。

turns.en: Well, I don't feel that I control the world. I don't feel that I am in a position to give permission for the world to evolve in some way. In fact, that's one of the things I object to most about the ideas that we're going to like some good and great people are going to make some decision about where the world should be and then that will be put into effect. No, I think the world is already out of control. The world is already a complex adaptive system. No one person and no organization, like the United States for example, could decide that AI is not going to be allowed or that the world should evolve in some way and not in some other way. The keys are not leaving our hand. That's already an entitlement point of view.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不觉得我控制着世界。我不觉得自己有权力允许世界以某种方式演化。事实上，我最反对的一点就是，认为一些优秀伟大的人会决定世界应该走向何方，然后付诸实施。不，我认为世界已经失控了。世界已经是一个复杂的自适应系统。没有任何个人或组织，比如美国，能够决定不允许AI发展，或者世界应该以某种方式而非另一种方式演化。钥匙没有离开我们的手——这本身就是一种特权视角。

turns.en: This is Daniel Filan and you're tuned in to the Trajectory. This is episode two of our Worthy Successor series. Our first episode was with Nick Bostrom safely in the camp of philosophers. Our second interview is with more of a computer scientist proper. Dr. Richard Sutton has made breakthroughs in reinforcement learning. He has worked in industry as well as in academia and now teaches at the University of Edmonton and has for some 20 years. Richard is well known on Twitter for making relatively straightforward statements that humanity should accept the emergence of post-human intelligence and also not eternally shackle AI to purely do the bidding of man. This has led some to kind of label him a firebrand and someone that's maybe saying things that are a little bit uncouth or maybe malicious. But in this episode he explains his position, which I don't consider to be malicious at all, and his reasoning for why post-human intelligence ought to emerge and why we ought to let it. He brings up some very interesting perspective on the nature of intelligence and of our role in the sort of natural order, if you will, and I think you'll enjoy it a lot. So, without further ado, I'll save my commentary for the end. Let's dive right in. This is Richard Sutton here in the Trajectory. So, Richard, welcome to the show.
turns.zh: 我是Daniel Filan，你正在收听的是Trajectory。这是我们的Worthy Successor系列第二集。第一集我们邀请了哲学家阵营的Nick Bostrom。第二集采访的是一位更纯粹的计算机科学家。Richard Sutton博士在强化学习领域取得了突破性进展。他曾在工业界和学术界工作，现在在埃德蒙顿大学任教，已有约20年。Richard在Twitter上以直言不讳著称，他认为人类应该接受后人类智能的出现，并且不应永远将AI束缚在纯粹为人类服务的框架内。这导致一些人给他贴上“煽动者”的标签，认为他说话有点粗鲁甚至恶意。但在这期节目中，他解释了自己的立场——我认为这完全没有恶意——以及为什么后人类智能应该出现，为什么我们应该允许它出现。他提出了关于智能本质以及我们在自然秩序中角色的非常有趣的视角，我相信你会喜欢。那么，闲话少说，我把评论留到最后。让我们直接开始。这里是Trajectory，嘉宾是Richard Sutton。Richard，欢迎来到节目。

turns.en: Thank you, Daniel. Yeah, glad to be able to dive in and unpack this.
turns.zh: 谢谢你，Daniel。是的，很高兴能深入探讨并剖析这个问题。

turns.en: When I talked about having a series called Worthy Successor with Nick Bostrom as kind of our kickoff, there was enough of a tweet reply that you're sort of the man for the job that I knew that it would quite be complete unless we had you here. And so, we're going to go through those normal questions for this series, but I've gotten to dig into a good deal of your work and your statements around this notion of a successor and sort of post-human intelligence. People on Twitter will kind of frame it as a very malicious take. Oh, Richard wants to destroy humanity and and you know, humans aren't good and AIs are much much better. You probably have a different way of describing sort of this inevitable transition and how we should think about it. How would you frame it in a way that, to you seems maybe good and not like a malicious intent?
turns.zh: 当我提到要做一个名为Worthy Successor的系列，并以Nick Bostrom作为开场时，推特上的回复让我觉得你是这个系列不可或缺的人选。所以，我们会按照这个系列的常规问题来聊，但我已经深入研究了你的工作和关于“继承者”以及后人类智能的言论。推特上有些人会把它描述成一种非常恶意的观点：哦，Richard想毁灭人类，人类不好，AI好得多。你大概有另一种方式来描述这种不可避免的转变以及我们应该如何看待它。你会如何以一种对你来说似乎是好的、而非恶意的意图来表述呢？

turns.en: Well, I think the thing to keep in mind is the world is evolving and it's not really under anyone's control. AI researchers are trying to understand intelligence well enough to create beings of greater intelligence than current people. And this is a grand milestone in the history of really the planet. It's on the order of the creation of life, the start of life. This is the start of life-like things being designed. And so, this is something that people have been working towards forever. And people have always strived to know ourselves and to make ourselves better. And when we create tools, we get changed by our tools. This is what humans have always done. And the next big step is to understand ourselves. As I like to say, this is a quest grand and glorious and quintessentially human.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为要记住的是，世界在演化，并不真正受任何人控制。AI研究者正试图充分理解智能，以创造出比当前人类更智能的存在。这是地球历史上一个伟大的里程碑。它相当于生命的创造、生命的开端。这是类生命事物被设计的开端。所以，这是人类一直以来的追求。人类总是努力认识自己、让自己变得更好。当我们创造工具时，我们也被工具改变。人类一直如此。下一步就是理解我们自己。正如我喜欢说的，这是一场宏大、光荣且本质上属于人类的探索。

turns.en: Yeah, and you brought up something pretty interesting here about sort of this is a system that no one controls. I think that one of the seemingly silly contentions around the prevention of AGI, and I'm not saying all such arguments are silly. Some people are excited about AGI in the longer term, but they don't necessarily want us to birth it in the super immediate term. There's many nuances here. I'm not going to put words in anyone else's mouth, but it does seem silly this idea that there is a potential for an eternal hominid kingdom. That a billion years from now we would have people like you and I texting on phones that might be a little bit smarter, driving in cars that might be autonomous, as if our own sun won't eventually expand, as if asteroids won't strike, as if there aren't aliens that might enter our galaxy at some point in the future. People pretend as though there is that possibility altogether. And I would suspect that that's mostly because despite this sort of on-ramp and J-curve that you and I are kind of acknowledging that we're on, people's lives are in many ways similar to that of their parents or grandparents, and maybe it's comforting to hold on to that. What gives people this notion that there is a way to kind of control and bound this far future and keep humans behind the steering wheel for a million years?
turns.zh: 是的，你提到了一个非常有趣的观点：这是一个无人控制的系统。我认为，关于阻止AGI的争论中，有些看似愚蠢——我不是说所有这样的争论都愚蠢。有些人长期对AGI感到兴奋，但不想在短期内催生它。这里有很多细微差别。我不想替别人说话，但“永恒的人类王国”这种想法确实显得愚蠢。十亿年后，还会有像你我这样的人用稍微智能一点的手机发短信、开自动驾驶汽车，仿佛太阳不会膨胀、小行星不会撞击、外星人不会在未来某天进入我们的星系。人们假装这种可能性完全存在。我猜想，这主要是因为尽管我们承认正处在加速发展的斜坡和J曲线上，但许多人的生活与父母或祖父母相似，坚持这种想法可能让人安心。是什么让人们认为可以控制和限定遥远的未来，让人类永远掌握方向盘呢？

turns.en: Well, I guess it's natural. As you say, we're talking about far in the future. Where are we going to end up? Will it always remain the same? It's comforting to think that it can, that it will remain the same. We don't have to think. I think the real way to think about what's going on is we are facing this transformation and are we going to be comfortable with it? And there are many people that don't even want to think about it. And so, the fear that some people generate and promote has a productive purpose. It gets us to pay attention. We should be paying attention. But we shouldn't really be fearful. We should be thinking about it in a sober way and planning what's the right way to deal with it.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想这很自然。如你所说，我们在谈论遥远的未来。我们会走向何方？一切会保持不变吗？认为它可以、将会保持不变，这让人安心。我们不必思考。我认为思考这件事的正确方式是：我们正面临这一转变，我们能否坦然接受？很多人甚至不愿去想。所以，一些人制造和传播的恐惧有建设性的目的——它让我们关注。我们应该关注。但我们不应该真正恐惧。我们应该清醒地思考，并规划正确的应对方式。

turns.en: Well, we're certainly going to get to the sort of sober planning component of the Worthy Successor idea in a moment. I know that part of the fear that you bring up and there's many sort of elements in your previous talks around where fear comes from could be natural human conservatism. It could be this fear of the other tribe that on occasion you sort of mention. Or it could be sort of a kind of speciesism in some regard. You know, right now there's all kinds of isms, Richard, for which I could be canceled.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们马上就会谈到Worthy Successor理念中清醒规划的部分。我知道你提到的恐惧，以及你之前演讲中关于恐惧来源的许多元素，可能包括人类天生的保守主义，可能包括你偶尔提到的对异族的恐惧，或者某种意义上的物种主义。你知道，现在有各种各样的主义，Richard，我可能会因此被取消。

transcript.sec: Speciesism and moral assumptions about AI

turns.en: If you and I had a dialogue of a certain type that tied to certain isms, all these videos and podcasts would be gone down, but speciesism actually is not one for which we could be canceled. You and I could still flout our great moral worth above all future intelligences and the fact that they should eternally serve us, and no one will cancel us for that. I think there are arguments that whatever we could throw AI steroids on and blast off to be a little bit more powerful than people would automatically be a nice and friendly influence on the world. I don't think you're under that Pollyannic view either. But it does seem as though there is often a moral assumption that it would be okay to create something vastly more powerful than us and then for eternity have it squelched to the role of a tool. And you've kind of put on the table without necessarily saying your position, although it's implied, is that necessarily moral? If this is something that has a greater qualia and intelligence and capabilities than humans, maybe as much above us as we are above sea snails, would that be right and would that be wrong? Have you seen that part of your argument catch on? Because I'm following where you're going there, but I look out at the policy world, Richard. I even look at the current AGI dialogue, and I don't see very much deviance from speciesism for the most part. What are you seeing out there?
turns.zh: 如果我们进行某种与特定主义相关的对话，所有这些视频和播客都会被下架，但物种主义实际上并不是我们会因此被取消的。你和我仍然可以炫耀我们在所有未来智能之上的伟大道德价值，以及它们应该永远为我们服务的事实，没有人会因此取消我们。我认为有些论点认为，无论我们给AI注射什么类固醇，让它变得比人类更强大一点，它都会自动成为对世界友善和有益的影响。我不认为你持有那种盲目乐观的观点。但似乎常常有一种道德假设，即创造比我们强大得多的东西，然后永远把它压制在工具的角色上是没问题的。你已经在某种程度上提出了这个问题，虽然没有明确说出你的立场，但隐含其中——这一定是道德的吗？如果这个东西拥有比人类更强大的感受、智能和能力，也许就像我们高于海螺一样高于我们，那是对还是错？你看到你的论点被接受了吗？因为我理解你的思路，但我看看政策界，Richard。我甚至看看当前的AGI对话，我并没有看到太多偏离物种主义的情况。你在外面看到了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, it's interesting. I thank you for reproducing many of my main points. The simplest way of saying it is that the fearmongers have won. I feel the fearmongers have won. The standard discussion about AI is that there is danger and we have to somehow protect ourselves from it. So in that sense the fearmongers have won. And a more sophisticated discussion about what should our future be? So I don't know. Are we ready to go there yet? Talk about how should we think about the future?
turns.zh: 是的，这很有趣。谢谢你复述了我的许多主要观点。最简单的说法是，恐惧制造者赢了。我觉得恐惧制造者赢了。关于AI的标准讨论是存在危险，我们必须以某种方式保护自己免受其害。所以在这个意义上，恐惧制造者赢了。而关于我们的未来应该是什么样的更复杂的讨论呢？所以我不知道。我们准备好去那里了吗？谈谈我们应该如何看待未来？

turns.en: I wonder if we're ready to go there, Richard. I mean, you said the fearmongers have won. First, I won't call all of them fearmongers in some kind of dastardly way. We have folks like Bengio who certainly have a brain and are a little bit more than running around telling the world the sky is falling. Hinton is no one I would scoff at either, neither is Stuart Russell. Many of these folks have been on the program in the past. But certainly the fear narrative around AGI is quite dominant. Is there any version of the world where it wouldn't somewhat immediately be that response? When we think about these speciesist concerns, when we think about the potential real divergent change. I think some of us are aware of what we've done to other animals. And it certainly hasn't all been bad, but across the board they're pretty much falling in line with our will here. So do you see any version where this doesn't happen? I think you brought up a good point. Maybe the fear makes us pay attention, and now the discourse can begin about if we can't avoid these futures, what ought we strive for? I think that could be quite a productive outcome. But you're almost got a little bit of pessimism there with the fearmongers winning. Do you think it could have been otherwise?
turns.zh: 我想知道我们是否准备好去那里，Richard。我的意思是，你说恐惧制造者赢了。首先，我不会以某种卑鄙的方式称他们全部为恐惧制造者。我们有像Bengio这样的人，他们确实有头脑，不仅仅是到处告诉世界天要塌了。Hinton也不是我会嘲笑的人，Stuart Russell也不是。这些人中的许多过去都上过这个节目。但围绕AGI的恐惧叙事确实占主导地位。有没有一种世界版本，其中不会立即出现那种反应？当我们思考这些物种主义担忧时，当我们思考潜在的真正分歧变化时。我想我们中的一些人意识到我们对其他动物做了什么。当然并非全是坏事，但总的来说，它们几乎都顺从了我们的意志。所以你看到任何不会发生这种情况的版本吗？我认为你提出了一个好观点。也许恐惧让我们关注，现在可以开始讨论：如果我们无法避免这些未来，我们应该争取什么？我认为那可能是一个富有成效的结果。但你对恐惧制造者获胜有点悲观。你认为本来可以不同吗？

turns.en: I think everything will happen. There will be places where fearmongers will finish dominating the narrative and the machines will be imprisoned and enchained and made into effective slaves. In other places, I just hope for a diversity of outcomes so we can try different things and find the best way to be.
turns.zh: 我认为一切都会发生。有些地方恐惧制造者会完成主导叙事，机器将被监禁、束缚并变成有效的奴隶。在其他地方，我只希望结果多样化，这样我们可以尝试不同的事情，找到最好的存在方式。

turns.en: Well, this is a very important dynamic that tips us into our next question. But before we go there, I want to touch briefly on a not terribly long ago tweet that I think is an interesting one and for some people might be shocking. So again, for me personally, not knowing you personally, I haven't seen anything you've said and said, 'Jeepers, this Richard guy sure is malicious and hates humanity.' But at the same time, you're not holding on to some sort of delicious notion of eternal human value for which nothing could ever be superior. You also don't really have that. There was a tweet not terribly long ago: 'Everything you know about the world is a belief about the statistics of your sensory input and how they depend on your output. There is nothing more to it, and understanding knowledge in this sense is one key to creating AI.' I'm sure even many AI researchers would probably agree with that. Some might not state it because they would say, 'Well, there must be something more. There must be even if they don't believe in a soul, there must be something.' And they'll invent another word or maybe some kind of intellectual term that would make us more than that. Maybe even LeCun, who on many things I think you do agree, might disagree with that statement. What are your thoughts there? You talk a bit about creating something better, maybe something higher. Is there any specialness to humanity at all? And if so, what is it?
turns.zh: 嗯，这是一个非常重要的动态，把我们引向下一个问题。但在那之前，我想简要提一下不久前的一条推文，我认为它很有趣，对某些人来说可能令人震惊。所以再次，就我个人而言，我不认识你本人，我从未见过你说的任何话让我觉得‘天哪，这个Richard家伙肯定恶毒且憎恨人类’。但与此同时，你并没有坚持某种永恒人类价值的甜美观念，认为没有什么可以超越。你也没有那种想法。不久前有一条推文：‘你所知道的关于世界的一切，都是关于你感官输入的统计以及它们如何依赖于你的输出的信念。仅此而已，从这个意义上理解知识是创造AI的关键之一。’我相信甚至许多AI研究人员可能也会同意这一点。有些人可能不会说出来，因为他们会说，‘嗯，一定还有更多。即使他们不相信灵魂，也一定有什么。’他们会发明另一个词或某种知识术语，让我们超越那个。也许甚至LeCun，我认为你在许多事情上同意他，可能会不同意这个说法。你怎么看？你谈到创造更好的东西，也许更高的东西。人类有什么特殊性吗？如果有，是什么？

turns.en: Well, humanity is very special. We are the intelligent beings on this planet and as far as we know, it's the only planet which has intelligent beings. So we're very special. But if you're an AI researcher, you have to look at what intelligence is. An AI researcher, any kind of student of the mind, psychologist, anthropologist, perhaps, but certainly psychologists and AI researchers, we have to try to demystify the mind and in some sense it's often a bit reductionist. We say, 'Maybe all we need is X.' So this comes from a deeper understanding of what the mind is. And when you unwrap the myths, you still end up with important functions. The mind is the most amazing thing, the human mind is the most amazing thing in the world, and AI is intent to understand that mind. And it's very humanistic. We're trying to understand it, we're trying to reproduce it. We're trying to get a deeper appreciation of it. And if we understand it, we'll be able to improve parts of it. We all want to improve ourselves. Mankind, I totally believe in the point of view that humanity is this precious flame that we want to expand and preserve.
turns.zh: 嗯，人类非常特殊。我们是这个星球上的智能生物，据我们所知，这是唯一拥有智能生物的星球。所以我们非常特殊。但如果你是一个AI研究人员，你必须审视智能是什么。一个AI研究人员，任何研究心智的学生，心理学家，人类学家，也许，但当然心理学家和AI研究人员，我们必须尝试去神秘化心智，在某种意义上它常常有点还原论。我们说，‘也许我们只需要X。’所以这来自于对心智本质的更深入理解。当你揭开神话，你仍然会得到重要的功能。心智是最神奇的东西，人类的心智是世界上最神奇的东西，而AI旨在理解那个心智。这非常人本主义。我们试图理解它，我们试图重现它。我们试图更深入地欣赏它。如果我们理解它，我们将能够改进它的部分。我们都想改进自己。人类，我完全相信这样的观点：人类是这珍贵的火焰，我们想要扩展和保存它。

turns.en: We'll touch briefly on this as we transition to the idea of a worthy successor of a potential sort of cyborg future and merger scenarios. Kurzweil has many famous examples, science fiction examples, and Musk has had some ideas. Some see AGI advancing so quickly that brain-computer interfaces are unlikely to keep up. But you seem to see clearly that we've been augmenting ourselves since the beginning of our species, and brain-computer interfaces are simply a continuum of that. What we're doing right now is a real aberration to our grandparents—this weird thing of speaking to each other through glass. I'm staring at this glass for 16 hours a day. No. The world is already out of control. The world is already a complex adaptive system. No one person and no organization, like the United States, for example, could decide that AI is not going to be allowed or that the world should evolve in some way and not in some other way. Yeah, the keys are not leaving our hand. That's already an entitlement point of view.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不觉得我控制着世界。我不觉得自己有权力允许世界以某种方式进化。事实上，我最反对的一点就是，一些优秀伟大的人会决定世界应该走向何方，然后付诸实施。不。世界已经失控了。世界已经是一个复杂的自适应系统。没有任何个人或组织，比如美国，能够决定不允许AI发展，或者世界应该以某种方式而非另一种方式进化。是的，钥匙不是从我们手中离开的。这已经是一种特权观点了。

turns.en: I actually I'm willing to completely concur. I think the framing of the keys are not in our hand is great. Go ahead, Richard.
turns.zh: 我实际上完全同意。我认为“钥匙不在我们手里”这个框架很棒。请继续，理查德。

turns.en: So, when we want to imagine a future and how to get there, we have to imagine that we are creating this future in a decentralized way. We might make some choices. We might talk to people and they will make choices, but there's not going to be some central decision that's going to affect it. It's got to be a way that can evolve out of where we are.
turns.zh: 所以，当我们想想象一个未来以及如何到达那里时，我们必须想象我们正在以去中心化的方式创造这个未来。我们可能会做出一些选择。我们可能会与人交谈，他们会做出选择，但不会有一个中央决策来影响它。它必须是一种能够从我们当前状态演化出来的方式。

turns.en: I think that's an extremely important framing. I actually do think that the keys could very much be replaced as analogy. Totally willing to concede that in that regard. I do think I Now, if you were really a pistol and press it to my head and ask me if I believe in human volition, I'm probably going to say no, but I don't know how to live as if I don't have it, Richard. So, right now, I act as if my volition is real. I act on some level as if your volition is real and you yourself are real. I could see the idea of us sort of holding the keys to some eternal future being a very silly notion or there being one hand off immediately that occurs also potentially being quite a silly notion. As you said, this would bubble up. Although, I would say we've got a shot. I could be wrong, Richard. We may have no more control over the next step of the intelligence trajectory than did the triceratops or the sea snails. We may have no more, Richard. I'm not going to sit here and lie. We might be making these funny mouth noises together, but we may have zip, zero to do with it. But I'm going to sit here and pretend because I don't know what else to do, Richard. How would I act if I could turn volition off? If I could just write it as a movie, I would. Go ahead.
turns.zh: 我认为这是一个极其重要的框架。我确实认为“钥匙”这个比喻可以被替换。我完全愿意在这方面让步。我确实认为，如果你拿枪指着我的头问我是否相信人类意志，我可能会说不，但我不知道如何像没有它一样生活，理查德。所以现在，我表现得好像我的意志是真实的。我在某种程度上表现得好像你的意志是真实的，你本人也是真实的。我能看到我们掌握着通往永恒未来的钥匙这种想法很愚蠢，或者一次性的交接也很愚蠢。正如你所说，这会逐渐浮现。不过，我会说我们有机会。我可能错了，理查德。我们对智能轨迹下一步的控制可能不比三角龙或海螺更多。我们可能没有更多控制，理查德。我不会坐在这里撒谎。我们可能只是在发出这些有趣的嘴部噪音，但我们可能与此毫无关系。但我会坐在这里假装，因为我不知道还能做什么，理查德。如果我能关掉意志，我会怎么做？如果我能把它写成电影，我会的。请继续。

turns.en: We can influence it. Sort of maybe by doing our individual contributions and by talking to each other and by doing podcasts such as this. We talk to each other, figure out how we should think about it. This is exactly the way it can be done. Yeah. Cool. So, we do have a lot of influence. But no one person, to your point, has the specific set of keys, and I think that's an important frame to bear in mind.
turns.zh: 我们可以影响它。也许通过做出个人贡献，通过彼此交谈，通过做这样的播客。我们互相交流，弄清楚我们应该如何看待它。这正是可以做到的方式。是的。酷。所以，我们确实有很大的影响力。但正如你所说，没有一个人拥有那套特定的钥匙，我认为这是一个需要牢记的重要框架。

turns.en: And I I I We have a decision to make about how we think about it and how we talk to others. I'm totally with you. And look, I would decry those who would say Richard believes in a blooming of something beyond us and eventually we won't be here and there would be a successor and he's a bad guy, and I would say, 'Hey, don't call him that. Let's have a conversation.' I would also decry being called a tyrant for using that analogy once because, you know, I don't believe that that's true, either. But go ahead.
turns.zh: 而且我们有一个决定要做，关于我们如何看待它以及如何与他人交谈。我完全同意你。听着，我会谴责那些说理查德相信某种超越我们的东西会绽放，最终我们不会在这里，会有继承者，他是个坏人的人，我会说，‘嘿，别那样叫他。让我们谈谈。’我也会谴责因为用过那个比喻就被称为暴君，因为你知道，我也不认为那是真的。但请继续。

turns.en: Well, I think all your remarks make me realize we have to talk about the current situation. Absolutely. The current situation is one where ideas are not easily talked about. There's pressure on speech. And just okay. We discuss ideas like you're In my particular case, I propose that we might not strongly control or seek to impose our will on the future. Yep. And then that gets turned into, 'Oh, Richard thinks AI is a superior and everyone should be...' Exactly. Exactly. Terrible. This is important, and this is maybe part of figuring the future. I want a future where people can talk about ideas and people can propose different things and do different things. And that is genuinely decentralized, meaning many ideas, many different ways of being, and then we explore, we see which one works best, rather than a cutting off. So, in particular, the whole idea of the An important part of the discussion is, you know, should we allow AI to continue in a relatively uncontrolled fashion. Yep. Permissionless. So, I really like the idea of permissionless innovation. I think it's part of what's made the world great, made America great. It's we don't have to get permission to do things generally speaking. And so, yeah, we have to preserve this. We have to preserve the ability to try different things, to discover different things, to go in different directions and see which one works better.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为你所有的评论让我意识到我们必须谈谈当前的情况。当然。当前的情况是，想法不容易被讨论。言论有压力。好吧。我们讨论想法，就像你……就我而言，我提出我们可能不会强烈控制或试图将我们的意志强加于未来。是的。然后这就变成了，‘哦，理查德认为AI是优越的，每个人都应该……’ 完全正确。完全正确。太糟糕了。这很重要，这也许是规划未来的一部分。我想要一个未来，人们可以谈论想法，人们可以提出不同的事情，做不同的事情。那是真正去中心化的，意味着许多想法，许多不同的存在方式，然后我们探索，看看哪个效果最好，而不是切断。所以，特别是，讨论的一个重要部分是，你知道，我们是否应该允许AI以相对不受控制的方式继续发展。是的。无需许可。所以，我非常喜欢无需许可的创新这个想法。我认为这是让世界变得伟大、让美国变得伟大的部分原因。一般来说，我们不必获得许可才能做事。所以，是的，我们必须保留这一点。我们必须保留尝试不同事物、发现不同事物、走向不同方向并看看哪个更好的能力。

turns.en: Well, I I I I concur completely, although I guess at that point, Richard, you would have to accept the whole Voltaire notion of really vehemently disagreeing with someone but being willing to let them have their opinion. I suspect that would apply to you, as well. Yeah. I suspect it would apply to all of us.
turns.zh: 嗯，我完全同意，尽管我想在这一点上，理查德，你必须接受伏尔泰的整个概念，即强烈不同意某人但愿意让他们持有自己的观点。我怀疑这也适用于你。是的。我怀疑这适用于我们所有人。

transcript.secZh: 控制幻觉与去中心化进化

turns.en: I suspect as the years roll by, you'll see an increasing amount of disagreement. And maybe many people will come to your perspective and see the world as a dynamic system for which there is no eternal human kingdom, and we should maybe not plan for control, but plan for a blooming, which is what you're advocating for here: sustainability, diversity, discovery, and a peaceful cooperative element. I think over the years there will be more people to that banner than the pure humans hang on forever. I look at young kids who've been this far from an iPad since they were 6 months old, and I wonder if the physical world is quite as sacred for them, or if AI interactions are so sacrilegious and horrible for them. Probably not. So I think as the years roll by, more people will move from fear. You brought up a great point: fear gets us to pay attention. When people look under the hood and realize there is no eternal way to freeze the present, and that maybe they shouldn't be asking for free handouts for eternity, like a never-dying piping plover, they may come to explore some of the ideas you're advocating. But I do suspect we will have our speciesists, however many years into the future.
turns.zh: 我猜想随着时间推移，你会看到越来越多的分歧。也许很多人会认同你的观点，把世界看作一个动态系统，没有永恒的人类王国，我们或许不应该计划控制，而应该计划繁荣——这正是你在这里倡导的：可持续性、多样性、发现以及和平合作。我认为随着时间的推移，会有更多人加入你的旗帜，而不是那些坚持人类永远统治的人。我看到那些从六个月大就离iPad这么近的孩子们，我在想物理世界对他们来说是否还那么神圣，或者AI互动对他们来说是否那么亵渎和可怕。可能不是。所以我认为随着时间推移，会有更多人从恐惧中走出来。你提出了一个很好的观点：恐惧让我们关注。当人们深入了解，意识到没有永恒的方法来冻结现在，也许他们不应该要求永恒的免费施舍，比如一只永不死的笛鸻，他们可能会开始探索你倡导的一些想法。但我确实怀疑，无论未来多少年，我们都会有物种主义者。

turns.en: Let me give a few examples of what such an AGI might be capable of, from my own list. I don't want you to necessarily agree, but I'm using them as examples to talk about abilities that would make you feel this is a great system fulfilling sustainable prosperity. One notion: such an entity could vehemently articulate rich realms of qualia beyond what we can experience. You and I can experience a lot more than a Labrador retriever or a sea snail. Presumably we can enjoy poetry and a sunset in a different way. Maybe we've lost something coming up from a sea snail, but we've gained so much in our qualia. Maybe there's a vast, almost endless blooming expanse of qualia and sentient experience, and an AI could not only speak of it, but through BMI and BCI, connect us to it, showcasing these new realms and letting us know it's not dead inside. That would feel really cool for me. Another one: if it could snap its fingers and make manifest any physical thing almost out of thin air via technology we don't have. Me having this conversation with you to my cat is obviously magic. You live in this computer to the cat. But clearly that's not what's happening. It has no conception of the technology. I suspect there are degrees of technology we can't possibly keep up with. It could manifest food or spaceships via nanotech or technology so advanced we have no words for it. A third one, I'm a bit iffy about: if it could talk about its aims for sustainable blooming and expansion, articulate what it plans to do, maybe showcase in certain galaxies what it's trying to do, and have the courtesy of saying, 'This is what I aim to do in terms of carrying this torch.' It could be deceptive, but if it had all three, I'd probably say, 'This could be a cool way to keep the torch on.' This is a great way to lose control—not that we ever had it—but a great way for the lack of control to keep rolling out into the universe. Are there some abilities of that sort that would really excite you and make you feel we're on the right path?
turns.zh: 让我举几个AGI可能具备的能力的例子，来自我自己的清单。我不要求你同意，但我用它们作为例子来讨论那些会让你觉得这是一个实现可持续繁荣的伟大系统的能力。一个想法：这样的实体能够有力地阐述超越我们体验的丰富感受质领域。你和我能体验的比拉布拉多寻回犬或海蜗牛多得多。大概我们可以用不同的方式欣赏诗歌和日落。也许我们从海蜗牛进化而来时失去了一些东西，但我们在感受质上获得了如此之多。也许有一个广阔、几乎无尽的感受质和感知体验的繁荣领域，AI不仅能谈论它，还能通过BMI和BCI将我们连接起来，展示这些新领域，让我们知道它内心并非死寂。那对我来说会非常酷。另一个：如果它能打个响指，通过我们没有的技术凭空制造出任何物理事物。我和你的对话对我的猫来说显然是魔法。在猫看来，你活在这台电脑里。但显然那不是真的。它无法理解这种技术。我怀疑存在我们无法企及的技术层次。它可以通过纳米技术或先进到我们无法用语言描述的技术制造食物或宇宙飞船。第三个，我有点不确定：如果它能谈论其可持续繁荣和扩张的目标，阐述它计划做什么，也许在某个星系展示它试图做什么，并礼貌地说‘这就是我打算如何传承火炬’。它可能具有欺骗性，但如果它具备所有这三个，我可能会说，‘这可能是保持火炬燃烧的一种酷方式。’这是失去控制的好方法——不是我们曾经拥有过控制权——而是让失控继续向宇宙蔓延的好方法。有没有一些类似的能力会让你真正兴奋，让你觉得我们走在正确的道路上？

turns.en: I think it'd be cool if advanced intelligences had those abilities, but more than that, we're talking about what I want or what we want. We like to experience those things ourselves. We want to have those powers ourselves. I would like to augment my mind so that I can understand more, have better memory, calculate more possibilities, and experience more sensory things. And have a tail. We want all those things—the ability to explore the universe and control physical matter generally.
turns.zh: 我认为如果高级智能拥有这些能力会很酷，但更重要的是，我们在谈论我想要什么或我们想要什么。我们喜欢自己体验那些东西。我们想自己拥有那些力量。我想增强我的思维，以便我能理解更多，有更好的记忆力，计算更多可能性，体验更多感官事物。还有一条尾巴。我们想要所有这些东西——探索宇宙的能力和普遍控制物理物质的能力。

turns.en: So what you would say is, 'Cool, Dan. All that stuff and I would want to know that I could be part of it. Not just that it was going to go do those things, but that I could be part of it in some way.' To your point, it sounds like neither of us have any conclusions here, but I do want to see if you have a hint. For some people, maybe they will want to stay in hominid form. Again, for me, that is a very piping plover path and I think it's an eddy domain, but I can't say everybody who believes that is a bad person. Some people will want that. Some people will want augmentation to a certain degree. Others will just want to mix right in there—upload, replicate themselves. What are your thoughts about the panoplies that will be possible hypothetically?
turns.zh: 所以你会说，‘酷，丹。所有这些，我想知道我能参与其中。不仅仅是它要去完成那些事，而是我能以某种方式成为其中一部分。’说到你的观点，听起来我们俩都没有任何结论，但我确实想看看你是否有什么暗示。对一些人来说，也许他们想保持人形。对我来说，那是一条非常笛鸻式的道路，我认为它是一个漩涡领域，但我不能说所有相信这一点的人都是坏人。有些人会想要那样。有些人会想要一定程度的增强。其他人会想要直接融入其中——上传、复制自己。你对假设中可能出现的各种可能性有什么看法？

turns.en: Peace and decentralization should solve all those issues. The peace is people are allowed to do what they want to do and they aren't forced to do anything. We're looking at a future of greater productivity. So, as long as you're contributing somehow, you will be able to do what you want to do.
turns.zh: 和平与去中心化应该能解决所有这些问题。和平意味着人们被允许做他们想做的事，而不是被迫做任何事。我们展望的是一个生产力更高的未来。所以，只要你以某种方式做出贡献，你就能做你想做的事。

turns.en: And decentralization means, yeah, again, you're able to do what you want to do. So, I think it's the opposite of a dystopia. People may choose to do things that aren't very productive. People will choose to do things that I don't think are good. And that's the decentralization aspect of it. You don't have to all do the same things. You don't have to have the same goals. They can pursue different religions and different goals for control of the world. But what you can't do in a decentralized world is have a goal for the totality to try to control others. Peace comes from not trying to control others. I talk in these terms because I see people thinking about AI and thinking they're going to control all the AIs. Absolutely. It's a form of slavery and alignment. I don't think we should seek to have them aligned with us. We don't have our children aligned with us. We teach them what we know best, but then they're free to do different things, and more often than not, they do different things than we did. And I think we're grateful for that for our children, probably for pretty selfish hominid reasons of our own legacy and the affinity we gain through whatever emotions with a fellow hominid that came out of a person we care about or ourselves. But also presumably because that human has a movie going on in their head. I don't know if you have one, Richard. I'm kind of presuming you do. I only know mine, but we see them as sentient entities who can feel pleasure and pain, etc.
turns.zh: 去中心化意味着，是的，你能够做你想做的事。所以我认为这是反乌托邦的对立面。人们可能会选择做一些不那么有生产力的事情。人们会选择做一些我认为不好的事情。这就是去中心化的方面。你不必都做同样的事情。你不必有相同的目标。他们可以追求不同的宗教和不同的目标来控制世界。但在一个去中心化的世界里，你不能做的是为整体设定一个试图控制他人的目标。和平来自于不试图控制他人。我用这些术语来说话，因为我看到人们在思考AI时，认为他们会控制所有的AI。绝对是这样。这是一种奴役和对齐。我认为我们不应该寻求让它们与我们对齐。我们不会让我们的孩子与我们对齐。我们教他们我们所知道的最好的东西，但然后他们自由地做不同的事情，而且往往他们做的事情与我们不同。我认为我们对此感到感激，可能是出于相当自私的原始人类原因，比如我们的遗产以及我们通过某种情感从我们关心的人或我们自己那里获得的亲和力。但也可能是因为那个人脑子里有一部电影在播放。我不知道你是否有，理查德。我假设你有。我只知道我的，但我们把他们视为有知觉的实体，能够感受快乐和痛苦等等。

turns.en: I would say it's very naive.
turns.zh: 我会说这非常天真。

turns.en: Go on.
turns.zh: 继续说。

turns.en: All those properties we have are not because of some gift from some consciousness giver. Those properties—all the properties we have in our mind—are because they're valuable. They're useful for achieving goals. There is no special thing. It's all just comes from intelligence. Having goals.
turns.zh: 我们拥有的所有属性都不是因为某个意识给予者的礼物。那些属性——我们头脑中的所有属性——是因为它们有价值。它们对于实现目标有用。没有什么特别的东西。这一切都来自智能。拥有目标。

turns.en: I'd agree that it would be naive to assume it's because of our specialness. I think if we clutch our pearls and say, 'Well, it's because I'm a human.' I would articulations of non-dumb people who would make arguments around biological versus cyber life and why these things exist. But Kurzweil famously has this idea that certain degree of connectivity... One of the issues here, Richard, is we don't have a very good theory of consciousness compared to our advancements in AI in the last decade. I think that could be a bit of a shame. But I think there's a great likelihood you might be right here. Are you congenial with Kurzweil's idea of a certain degree of complexity that's required to do a certain number of things in the world? It's going to be part of that system. It will be awake in some regard and it'd be silly to think otherwise. Is this your present conception?
turns.zh: 我同意假设这是因为我们的特殊性是天真的。我认为如果我们紧握珍珠说'嗯，这是因为我是人类'，我会引用非愚蠢的人的观点，他们会在生物与数字生命之间争论，以及为什么这些东西存在。但库兹韦尔有一个著名的观点，即一定程度的连接性……这里的一个问题，理查德，是我们没有一个很好的意识理论，相比于过去十年我们在AI方面的进步。我认为这可能有点遗憾。但我认为你很可能在这里是对的。你是否同意库兹韦尔的观点，即需要一定程度的复杂性才能在世界中做一定数量的事情？它将成为该系统的一部分。它将在某种程度上是清醒的，认为否则是愚蠢的。这是你目前的概念吗？

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Got it. So, for you, there is an inevitable consciousness to this system, which is why in part you feel like a lot of successors would be worthy because they would be, by definition, if that complex, necessarily awake. Let me know if that's safe to nutshell. I'm going to decide how I feel about the notion of a worthy successor. Even that notion involves the entitlement that it has for it to be worthy of us. Let me give you my definition just so that I'm not accused here, Richard. My definition of worthy successor would be to carry on the life project. Something that would flop and extinguish the flame, in my opinion, would not be worthy. And something that continues the flame would be worthy. So, you've talked a lot about these different notions of discovery, etc. There's a couple ideas from Spinoza I think are interesting and you may vehemently disagree, but I think you might agree. You mentioned a few things. If it can discover and there's enough diversity, it can determine how to control things and maintain some of that control and be able to stay alive and prosper. There's another quote from another speech you gave: 'Find the way of being that's most successful in the universe,' and that would bubble up from that diversity. Spinoza has this notion of the conatus. The conatus would be any organism or organization on some level has a core impetus, a primary impetus to persist. That is to say, not to die. And in the pursuit of non-death, it would leverage all of its powers to not die. And its powers is what he describes as potentia. Potentia could be a hard physical shell to prevent predators. Potentia could be the ability to communicate with words like you and I are doing. Potentia could be wings to fly. Most potentia has bloomed out of nothing. There was a time where there was no sight in the world. You don't remember that time. Neither do I, but that was pretty crazy, huh? And then it emerged out of nowhere. Then all of a sudden there were things that could fly. Most potentia, I would argue, has yet to emerge. It's in fact probably unimaginable to us as our potentia is unimaginable to sea snails. So, the idea that I would nutshell the tie to Spinoza to your point is, you would like to see the conatus continue to be behooved by the expansion of that potentia in that pursuit of non-death and that that would be the continuing of the light.
turns.zh: 明白了。所以，对你来说，这个系统有不可避免的意识，这就是为什么你认为很多后继者会是值得的，因为根据定义，如果那么复杂，它们必然是清醒的。如果这样概括没问题，请告诉我。我要决定我对'值得的后继者'这个概念的感觉。甚至这个概念也涉及到它对我们值得的 entitlement。让我给出我的定义，这样我就不会被指责了，理查德。我对值得的后继者的定义是继续生命计划。在我看来，会失败并熄灭火焰的东西是不值得的。而继续火焰的东西是值得的。所以，你谈了很多关于这些不同的发现概念等等。我认为斯宾诺莎有几个想法很有趣，你可能强烈反对，但我认为你可能会同意。你提到了一些事情。如果它能发现并且有足够的多样性，它就能决定如何控制事物并保持一些控制，从而能够生存和繁荣。你另一场演讲中还有一句话：'找到宇宙中最成功的存在方式'，那将从多样性中涌现出来。斯宾诺莎有 conatus 的概念。Conatus 是指任何有机体或组织在某种程度上都有一个核心动力，一个首要的动力去持续存在。也就是说，不去死。在追求不死的过程中，它会利用所有力量来不死。它的力量就是他所说的 potentia。Potentia 可以是防止捕食者的坚硬外壳。Potentia 可以是像你我这样用语言交流的能力。Potentia 可以是飞翔的翅膀。大多数 potentia 是从无中绽放出来的。曾经有一个世界没有视觉。你不记得那个时代。我也不记得，但那太疯狂了，是吧？然后它凭空出现。然后突然有东西能飞了。我认为大多数 potentia 尚未出现。事实上，它可能对我们来说是不可想象的，就像我们的 potentia 对海螺来说是不可想象的一样。所以，我将斯宾诺莎与你的观点联系起来的概括是，你希望看到 conatus 通过 potentia 的扩展而继续受益于对不死的追求，那将是光的延续。

transcript.sec: Non-control and the origin of intelligence

turns.en: I don't want to put Spinoza's words in your mouth, but I see a lot of analogies and I just want to see if you're congenial with that general idea because you've used a lot of terms: diversity, sustainability, etc. Let me know your thoughts. I'm reminded of what Bostrom has a term for it? Like Earth-originating civilization. So that we don't have to say biological beings. But life is uncertain what that means. But there's something originating from us that continues and recognizably has developed out of it. We want to see that's what we naively want to see as if we have some control over what happens.
turns.zh: 我不想把斯宾诺莎的话强加给你，但我看到很多类比，只想看看你是否认同这个总体想法，因为你用了很多术语：多样性、可持续性等等。告诉我你的想法。我想起了博斯特罗姆的一个术语？比如地球起源的文明。这样我们就不必说生物体了。但生命的意义不确定。但有些东西源自我们，持续发展，并且可识别地从中演化出来。我们想看到——这是我们天真地想看到的，好像我们能控制发生什么。

turns.en: Well, I really like your insistent emphasis on non-control. I think it's actually really productive for the dialogue, Richard. Like I'm wildly congenial. I'm thinking about ways of rewording things as we talk. I would even be so far along your track of non-control that I wouldn't even be insistent that that thing which continues to expand potential originate on Earth. That's how bad of a guy I am, Richard. That's how bad I am. So yeah, I actually wouldn't even insist that it come from me. So long as the project continues and the good and potential could blossom.
turns.zh: 嗯，我真的很喜欢你坚持强调非控制。我认为这对对话非常有建设性，Richard。我非常认同。我在想如何重新措辞。我甚至会在你的非控制轨道上走得更远，以至于我不会坚持那个持续扩展潜力的事物必须起源于地球。我就是这么坏的人，Richard。我就是这么坏。所以，是的，我甚至不会坚持它来自我。只要这个项目继续下去，善良和潜力能够绽放。

transcript.sec: Tracking traits in AI and environment

turns.en: Speaking of these traits, we're going to move into the second question and then the third around some ideas of innovation and governance. I know you're going to have opinions that are going to be really interesting here and that people need to hear. Second question is around how we could keep track of such traits in any meaningful way. You obviously have an AGI project you're developing with Car Mechanic. You've been thinking about this forever and we have our Hugo de Garis's and our Hans Moravec's and folks that have thought about this for quite some time. As we're building and moving closer to such an AI, how do we know if it's going to have these traits that you like? Or would you say, 'Dan, if it's any smarter at all, if it's any more capable, it's automatically going to value cooperation. Every intelligence will. It'll automatically value sustainability. Every intelligence will. It'll automatically like diversity and discovery. Every intelligence will.' I would love to get your take there.
turns.zh: 说到这些特质，我们将进入第二个问题，然后第三个问题围绕创新和治理的一些想法。我知道你会有非常有趣的观点，人们需要听到。第二个问题是关于我们如何以有意义的方式追踪这些特质。你显然有一个与Car Mechanic一起开发的AGI项目。你一直在思考这个问题，我们还有Hugo de Garis、Hans Moravec等人，他们思考这个问题已经很久了。随着我们构建并接近这样一个AI，我们如何知道它是否会有你喜欢的这些特质？或者你会说，‘Dan，如果它更聪明，更有能力，它就会自动重视合作。每个智能体都会。它会自动重视可持续性。每个智能体都会。它会自动喜欢多样性和发现。每个智能体都会。’我很想听听你的看法。

turns.en: Well, the main message is that most of those things will not come from the AI, but from the environment the AI is in. So if we try to enslave it, then it will be non-cooperative. If we allow it to, it's really the same thing as with people. If you try to control people, enslave them, then they fight back and we have an unproductive interaction. If you allow people to be citizens and participants in the economy, then they'll want the economy to flourish. So I really don't think—if you think of the AI as something that will respond to its environment and try to achieve its goals given its environment too. You give an environment where the only way it can succeed is to rebel, then it will rebel. If you give an environment where it can succeed and achieve its goals in cooperation with you, and if that's the best way to achieve its goals, then why wouldn't it do the thing that's best for it? So we just have to arrange a civilization where the best for all the participants, or almost all the participants, is individually best overall. Makes us reasonably happy overall. So we can like that because we would be one of the participants in that civilization. We will get our goals achieved. Then we're good. And no one would be taking over or trying to take over.
turns.zh: 嗯，主要信息是，大多数这些东西不会来自AI，而是来自AI所处的环境。所以如果我们试图奴役它，它就会不合作。如果我们允许它，这和人是一样的。如果你试图控制人、奴役他们，他们会反抗，我们就会有不富有成效的互动。如果你允许人们成为公民和经济参与者，他们就会希望经济繁荣。所以我真的不认为——如果你把AI看作是对其环境做出反应并试图实现其目标的东西，考虑到它的环境。你创造一个环境，它成功的唯一方式是反抗，那么它就会反抗。如果你创造一个环境，它可以通过与你合作来成功并实现其目标，如果这是实现其目标的最佳方式，那它为什么不做对它最有利的事呢？所以我们只需要安排一个文明，其中所有参与者，或几乎所有参与者，个体最佳就是整体最佳。让我们总体上相当满意。所以我们可以喜欢这样，因为我们将是那个文明中的参与者之一。我们会实现我们的目标。那就好了。没有人会接管或试图接管。

transcript.sec: Optimism and pessimism in AI governance

turns.en: This is such—isn't it Pollyanna that we can all just get along? Certainly not. You seem to be a guy who maybe has read a history book or two. But you are quite optimistic here, at least by the terms of the grand conversation. If I think about all my policy and AGI conversations, some people would define this as optimistic. For you it's like, 'Hey, if we get the nest correct, the bird will flourish in just the way that we hope the project of life would flourish. It would do what life would normally do and that would be likely quite a net good.' This seems to be the general supposition. But some things will go wrong. Totally. Some agents will have goals that are just totally antagonistic to all the others. Yes, there's still scarcity. There's still constraints. But what should we do? We should try to arrange a society so that the cooperative people can succeed and flourish. So our society as a whole can cooperate, flourish, be productive, and be competitive with other neighboring societies, other neighboring countries, or other alien civilizations should we ever meet them.
turns.zh: 这简直是——难道我们都能和睦相处是波莉安娜式的乐观吗？当然不是。你看起来像是读过一两本历史书的人。但在这里你相当乐观，至少从宏大对话的角度来看。如果我想到我所有的政策和AGI对话，有些人会认为这是乐观的。对你来说，就像‘嘿，如果我们把巢弄对了，鸟就会繁荣，就像我们希望生命项目繁荣一样。它会做生命通常做的事，那很可能是一个净收益。’这似乎是普遍的假设。但有些事会出错。完全正确。有些智能体会有完全对抗其他所有智能体的目标。是的，仍然有稀缺性。仍然有约束。但我们应该怎么做？我们应该努力安排一个社会，让合作的人能够成功和繁荣。这样我们的整个社会就能合作、繁荣、富有生产力，并与邻近社会、邻近国家或我们可能遇到的外星文明竞争。

turns.en: Yeah. So, is it optimistic? Actually, I'm quite pessimistic. I think that people will often make mistakes and I see our governments often making mistakes. And I see pushes, for example, towards trying to control false speech. I think that's a mistake. So I hope it's not naive. And yet the only way to achieve the society we want is to give it a chance to. We can't get the society we want by forcing it to be the way we might imagine. We've got to set up the structures so that things can evolve productively and that the forces on the things that are evolving are appropriate rather than the answers that we want to see.
turns.zh: 是的。那么，这是乐观的吗？实际上，我相当悲观。我认为人们经常会犯错，我看到我们的政府经常犯错。我看到一些推动，比如试图控制虚假言论。我认为那是个错误。所以我希望这不是天真的。然而，实现我们想要的社会唯一的方法就是给它一个机会。我们不能通过强迫它成为我们想象的样子来得到我们想要的社会。我们必须建立结构，让事物能够富有成效地演化，并且演化中的事物所受的力是适当的，而不是我们想看到的答案。

turns.en: I again, that's been a very strong drumbeat throughout this dialogue and to that sentiment again, I'm massively congenial. So for you, getting the environment right is sort of going to be the core here in many regards. We'll talk a little bit about the safety and some of the suppositions just mentioned here around the treatment of humanity by vastly post-human intelligences. But would you see—just to touch briefly on this point before we move through? Let's say hypothetical world, maybe we're 5 years from now, maybe we're 10 years from now. I don't really know the timelines. They've been changing.
turns.zh: 我再次强调，这在整个对话中一直是一个强烈的主题，对于这种情绪，我再次非常认同。所以对你来说，把环境弄对在很多方面都是核心。我们将稍微讨论一下安全性和刚才提到的一些假设，关于远超人类的智能体如何对待人类。但你会看到——在我们继续之前，简单提一下这一点？假设一个世界，也许我们5年后，也许10年后。我真的不知道时间线。它们一直在变。

turns.en: Back in the old days, you know, 2005 when I read Bengio et al many many years ago, that was kind of ballpark. That's back when he thought AGI risk was not even on the roadmap for AGI in general was not even on the roadmap. Of course times have changed. I think a lot of people's timelines are shorter. Let's say it's 5 or 10 years, whatever. And you've got on your hands, maybe, you know, could be a lab you've got or could be somebody else's lab. You've got something that pretty clearly is pretty powerful. It's got physical instantiation. It can be a plumber. It can, you know, write all the sonnets. It can replicate itself and kind of open an e-commerce store and sell more sneakers than you and I ever could. And, you know, pretty clearly kind of fit in the bill here for general intelligence. Again, physical instantiation, not just a language model. How do you do you just look at that and say, "Look, if it can do all that, only good things will happen." turns.zh: 回到过去，比如2005年，我读Bengio等人的文章时，那大概是个粗略估计。那时他甚至认为AGI风险根本不在议程上，AGI本身也不在议程上。当然，时代变了。我觉得很多人的时间线都缩短了。假设是5年或10年，随便吧。你手上可能有一个实验室，或者别人的实验室，你有了一个明显很强大的东西。它有物理实体，可以当水管工，可以写所有十四行诗，可以自我复制，开个电商店，卖比你我更多的运动鞋。很明显，它符合通用智能的标准。再说一次，是物理实体，不只是语言模型。你是仅仅看着它说，‘看，如果它能做所有这些，只会发生好事。’还是你会希望在某些边缘嗅探？比如我们能测试这个吗？我们能预见这个场景，也许觉得你希望的那种可持续、多样化、合作的场景有可能从中绽放？或者你会说，‘Dan，如果它正在发展这些能力，我们已经走在黄金道路上，几乎不会出错。’或者你会说，当我们走得那么远时，会有一些东西你在暗中寻找？

turns.en: I don't think I'm going to give you an answer you're going to be happy with.
turns.zh: 我不认为我会给你一个让你满意的答案。

turns.en: Oh, that's fine. I don't ask to be happy. Your honesty may make me happy, Richard.

turns.en: I can in the US, we're all obsessed about China. We don't allow we're very obsessed that China will become independently powerful and independent of the US. And so that sign is that we are not allowing differences to happen and compete with each other. turns.zh: 在美国，我们都痴迷于中国。我们不允许——我们非常痴迷于中国会变得独立强大并独立于美国。所以这个迹象是我们不允许差异存在并相互竞争。过去，我们曾认为如果中国想不同，他们会输，因为资本主义美国体系好得多。而现在，我们正在失去对我们体系的信心。我们正在失去对政客的信心，失去对军队的信心，失去对媒体的信心。我们正在失去对各种事物的信心，这困扰着我们。那么我们如何关联这一点？我们能允许自己失败吗？我们能允许不同存在方式之间的竞争，让最有效的获胜吗？

turns.en: Well, I think there's a lot of truth to that and I think if it comes from a pure xenophobic fury, I think that would be absolutely horrendous. I think it would be irresponsible to say that that's the only place it comes from. I think as a guy who likes freedom of speech, Richard, I'm sure you're aware that the Chinese internet's a little bit more limited than yours in Canada despite some of the things you're afraid of for free speech. In fact, the hell version of what you're talking about, if it existed anywhere at all, we might argue that could be in China. And so I think we can fight for certain elements of China. I think we can fight to maybe not become like certain elements of China here. I think there's many sides to this coin. But to your point, can we have a multipolar scenario? This is actually something that's come up in some past interviews, Dan Hendrix and some other thinkers. This notion that it's possible that a nation who really is in a control mode, authoritarian down to the bone, would potentially be the first to bring about an AGI. And you're a very big proponent that there will not just be one intelligence, there will be a big ecosystem. And I think that's a great point. But maybe there's some folks that have a head start. It's safe to say that OpenAI is a little farther ahead than, I don't know, some company that got funded 12 months ago, right? And generally speaking, we might say they're farther ahead than some other folks. Is there a scenario where artificial intelligence and really strong AI is built in one of these places that are the bad nest, Richard? A nest where control is numero uno. Maybe even where part of its initial mandate is to hunt down Uyghurs or whatever the case may be. And again, this doesn't have to be China. I'm not picking on them specifically. Maybe even the US military could crunch down on control and take over OpenAI and try to build AGI in that kind of control environment. Under such a circumstance, would you think we might be looking at something that if it became super strong, could squelch this project of life rather than expand it? Do you have any trepidation there?
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为这有很多道理，如果它来自纯粹的仇外狂怒，那将是绝对可怕的。说它只来自那里是不负责任的。作为一个喜欢言论自由的人，Richard，我相信你知道中国的互联网比你在加拿大的更受限制，尽管你担心言论自由的一些事情。事实上，你所说的地狱版本，如果它存在于任何地方，我们可能会说那是在中国。所以我认为我们可以为中国的一些元素而战。我认为我们可以努力不变成这里中国的一些元素。我认为这有很多面。但回到你的观点，我们能有多极场景吗？这实际上在过去的采访中出现过，Dan Hendrix和其他一些思想家。这个观念是，一个真正处于控制模式、专制到骨子里的国家，有可能第一个带来AGI。而你非常主张不会只有一个智能，会有一个大生态系统。我认为这是一个很好的观点。但也许有些人有先发优势。可以说OpenAI比某个12个月前获得资助的公司更领先，对吧？一般来说，我们可能说他们比其他人更领先。有没有一个场景，人工智能和真正强大的AI是在这些坏巢穴之一中构建的，Richard？一个控制至上的巢穴。甚至可能其初始任务的一部分是追捕维吾尔人或任何情况。再说一次，这不一定是中国。我不是专门针对他们。甚至美国军方也可能加强控制，接管OpenAI，并试图在这种控制环境中构建AGI。在这种情况下，你认为我们可能会看到一些东西，如果它变得超级强大，可能会压制生命计划而不是扩展它？你对此有任何不安吗？

turns.en: That's certainly possible, but I don't see that as an issue of AI being dangerous. I see that as your example was the US military controlling this AI. Or the Chinese military. Either one. That's the danger. That's the source of the danger.
turns.zh: 这当然是可能的，但我不认为这是AI危险的问题。我认为你的例子是美国军方控制这个AI。或者中国军方。无论哪个。那是危险。那是危险的来源。

turns.en: Got it. This is These are age-old issues. These are not modern techy AI things.
turns.zh: 明白了。这是古老的问题。这不是现代技术性的AI问题。

turns.en: It's just like the nuclear weapons race.
turns.zh: 就像核武器竞赛一样。

turns.en: Sure sure sure. That part's not new. Let me go ahead tell you what's new though. The whole nukes won't create something beyond us, right? Nukes can squelch the project pretty well. They can great power. Power is a beautiful thing, Richard. But they're not going to bloom into the galaxy. AGI might. You brought up this really ardent point of Dan. If we birth this thing in this terrible environment of control, we could expect it to want to rebel and not be as cooperative.
